musicalLanguage.description: Jerome Kern's All the Things You Are runs 71 measures at 92 BPM, and this is a case where the harmony genuinely resists any confident reading: the stated Ab major is strongly contradicted by the content (statedKeyFit -0.728), and the harness's own content-based guess, F# minor, carries essentially no signal (margin 0.016, below its own usability floor). What the 16 confidence-gated windows do show — every single one hedged as inferred harmony, and every one of the 16 different chord labels appearing exactly once, with none repeating — is a piece that moves through an unusually wide spread of harmonic color rather than settling on any tonal center; that scattering is itself the honest finding here, not a progression to be reconstructed. Structurally, the piece is genuinely through-composed: the raw repeat detector finds zero literal repeats anywhere, and even the transposition-aware pattern pass turns up only modest, heavily-transposed fragments. The left hand rests for a full 4 measures in the middle of the piece (measures 44-47) — the longest silence of either hand — while the piece as a whole opens and closes on complete rests (measures 1 and 71).
musicalLanguage.structure: The section lens finds 8 regions across the piece, with its three largest novelty spikes at measures 5, 30, and 53 (3.637, 3.507, and 3.964) roughly dividing the piece into thirds. The busiest measures — 30 (24 onsets), 61 (23), 21 (21), and 53 (21) — sit right at or near those same structural pivots, so the piece's harmonic and melodic activity intensifies exactly where the section lens marks a real turn. The 4-measure left-hand silence at measures 44-47 falls inside the section spanning measures 41-52, a comparatively still stretch between two of the piece's busier regions.
keyMoments: Every one of the 16 confidence-gated chord windows carries the inferred-harmony hedge, and every one of the 16 distinct labels appears exactly once, with none repeating; this scattering, not a hidden progression, is the honest harmonic picture the data supports and it teaches real caution about over-reading thin evidence.

Neither the stated Ab major (statedKeyFit -0.728) nor the content-detected F# minor (margin 0.016, below the tool's own usability floor) is trustworthy here — this teaches an important skill: recognizing when a piece's harmony is genuinely too fluid for either the stated key or a whole-song correlation to capture.

The left hand rests for a full 4 measures at measures 44-47 — the single longest rest gap of either hand in the piece — right in the middle of the harmonically busiest stretch, a deliberate textural clearing rather than a structural edge.
The piece opens and closes on complete silence (measure 1 and measure 71 both carry zero onsets in either hand), bookending 69 measures of continuous harmonic motion with matching stillness.
teachingGoals: Practice recognizing genuinely scattered, wide-ranging harmony as a real musical feature — with 16 different implied chord colors each appearing only once, resist forcing this piece into a single memorized progression.

Learn to identify structural pivots by density: the busiest measures (30, 61, 21, and 53) line up with the section lens's largest novelty spikes, so build technical control at those specific measures first.
Practice the 4-measure left-hand rest at measures 44-47 as a real musical event — recognize where the right hand carries the texture alone and plan the hand's re-entry deliberately.

Build a sense of the piece's overall arc from silence to silence (measure 1 to measure 71), understanding the harmonic restlessness in between as the substance of the piece, not a puzzle to resolve into one key.
styleTips: Play each of the many implied chord colors for its own character rather than trying to resolve them into a single tonal center — this piece's harmonic language moves too quickly and too widely for that approach to serve the music.

Listen for the moments where the harmony briefly turns dark (the diminished and augmented colors scattered through measures 10, 36, 37, 43, 57, and 63) as color, not error — voice them with the same commitment as the more familiar major and minor sonorities around them.

Try letting the right hand carry real weight alone through the 4-measure left-hand rest at measures 44-47, using the silence in the lower register to your advantage rather than treating it as a gap to fill.
Think of the piece's overall shape as silence-to-silence: use the complete rests at measures 1 and 71 to frame everything in between as one continuous harmonic journey.
